title: Why a Cancer Drug That Restores Differentiation Is Better Than One That Kills
color_semantics: TEAL = differentiated/mature cell/drug working/epigenetic lock released; CRIMSON = immature blast/2HG present/epigenetic lock engaged

metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no IDH biochemistry beyond 2HG-as-competitive-inhibitor concept; no comparison of IDH1 vs IDH2 inhibitors; no distinction between AML and glioma IDH mutations; no DNMT inhibitor mechanism; no discussion of EZH2 inhibitors

beats.narration_text: A leukemia patient starts a new drug. Weeks later the blast count falls. But here's the thing — the cancer cells did not die.

props.headline: On the blood smear: mature neutrophils with segmented nuclei — cells…

beats.narration_text: On the blood smear: mature neutrophils with segmented nuclei — cells that should not exist in this bone marrow.
Those neutrophils carry the original IDH1 mutation in their genome. They were the cancer. They grew up.
Cancer drugs are supposed to kill cancer cells. Here blast counts fall but the cells do not die — they mature. How does blocking a metabolic enzyme produce cells that finish differentiating?

Normal blood development runs one direction: a stem cell commits, matures through stages, and eventually dies of old age. The mature cell is the endpoint — that process is always running in healthy bone marrow.
In AML, that program stalls. Blasts — immature progenitors — accumulate because the differentiation gate is locked shut. The program is intact. It just cannot run.
Here is why the IDH1 mutation locks it. The mutation produces a metabolite called 2-hydroxyglutarate — 2HG. It closely resembles the molecule that demethylases need to erase methyl marks from DNA.
2HG jams the demethylases by competitive inhibition. Methyl marks that should be erased to open differentiation genes stay locked in place. The epigenetic lock holds — and the blast cannot mature.
Ivosidenib blocks the mutant IDH1 enzyme. 2HG levels fall. Demethylases recover. The methyl marks on differentiation genes are gradually erased — and the gate begins to open.

props.headline: The cell was always going to differentiate. The program was intact.…

beats.narration_text: The interesting part is this: the cell was always going to differentiate. The developmental program was intact. Only a chemical lock — 2HG — was preventing it from running.
Illustrative: day 0 — eighty percent blasts. Day 14 — bilobed nuclei appear. Day 28 — mature neutrophils refill the marrow. Day 56 — fewer than five percent blasts remain.
A metabolic drug removed a chemical lock, and a developmental program ran to completion. The cancer cells did not die. They finished growing up.
